From Cinema to the Self : Interconnectedness of Objects and Subjects

# From Cinema to the Self : Interconnectedness of Objects and Subjects

### How a conversation about Dhurandhar with my friend turned into a lesson on philosophy

# **Old Nyāya Wine in a New Bottle**

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

While the Nyāya school philosophers were arguing with the Buddhists, they realised a crucial interdependence between the objective realm and the subjective realm. Let me explain them using modern analogies which is based on a conversation I had with my friend about Dhurandhar.

# **Common Sense Realism**

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Imagine you are in the cinema hall watching the movie along with many other people. Common sense would tell you that the cinema is running and exists objectively, irrespective of whether anyone is watching it or not. We would agree on the following four notions.

### **Common Notions about Objects**

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

O1. External Independence : The movie running in the theatre is different from my experience of it that is running in my mind (the sights and sounds that I feel when I watch the movie)

O2. Substantivity : The person of Hamza Ali Mazari is distinct from the qualities that I see and hear about him. Hamza is not just an accidental collection of pixels that I see and wavelets that I hear. Hamza has a deep voice, is a fighter, and blah blah blah, but Hamza is a person who is more than a mere collection of his qualities. The qualities that I see and hear in the person of Hamza are properties of the person Hamza but Hamza is not simply reducible to a mere bunch of the things that I see and hear in him.

O3. Endurance across Sense and Time : The Hamza in Chapter one is the same person as the Hamza in Chapter four. The Hamza whose sound I hear when fighting is the same Hamza whom I see fighting. The Hamza whom I see from one angle is same as the Hamza I see from another angle when I change places after the intermission in the theatre.

O4. Publicness : The Dhurandhar movie I am watching is the same movie as the one which my friend is watching. The Hamza whom I perceive is also same that my friend is also perceiving.

Replace “hamza” by apples or any other object, the same thing holds. These common sense views that everyone holds is called as “common sense realism” in philosophy. That there exists same enduring objects out there in the external world independently which we all perceive independently.

### **Common Notions about the Self**

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

There are also these four common sense notions that we would agree on :

S1. Endurance across Time : I, who watched the movie, am the same person as the person who I was ten years ago, despite the fact that there is nothing common between the experiences I had in these two times.

S2. Endurance across Senses : If I hear the sound of Hamza Ali Mazari speaking and I think it is connected to the visual form of Hamza Ali Mazari whom I experienced at a previous time, as both sensations of the same object (despite disconnected in time and senses), I am the same person at both these times.

S3. Substantivity : I am distinct from any of my particular experiences that i have nor am I reducible to the collection of all my experiences in aggregate (I ≠ my life).

What the Nyaya philosophers saw was that both of these four objective common sense notions (O) and three subjective common sense notions (S) are epistemologically interconnected that denying one leads to the denial of the other, and affirmation of one leads to the affirmation of the other. The Buddhist denies realism in both the objective realm and the subjective realm (there are no enduring objects and there are no enduring persons as well). Let’s see how the argument is made.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!dvUs!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F50c8118c-86bc-4437-ad1d-12ae6d843ca9_400x225.jpeg)

![‘Ranveer Singh at all-time low, Aditya Dhar had 1 film’: Sanjay Gupta weighs in on the Rs 300 crore gamble behind ‘Dhurandhar’](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!dvUs!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F50c8118c-86bc-4437-ad1d-12ae6d843ca9_400x225.jpeg "Filmmaker Sanjay Gupta praised Aditya Dhar's vision behind 'Dhurandhar', calling it a massive risk given Ranveer Singh's career low and Dhar's limited filmmaking experience. He credited the Ambanis' backing as a key factor in the film's success. The two-part franchise has reportedly crossed Rs 3000 crore worldwide.")

# **Argument 1 : Objectivity implies Subjectivity**

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

First let us prove that affirming a common sense notion of objects implies the necessity to affirm a common sense notion of subjects. Or in other words, denying a common sense notion of subjects implies the necessity to deny a common sense notion of objects. Notion O1 says that the movie that you precieve in your head is different from the movie that actually plays out there. The Dhurandhar movie that is out there and runs on the theatre is different from your experience of it. So, there must be something that experiences, which is distinct from the thing that is experienced. Self-denialists like Buddhists or Hume tell that there is no such thing as a self and what we call as self is just the aggregate of momentary mental events. There is no permanent enduring self that possess all the experiences (that a person like me has) and exists over and above it. The aggregate of experiences are connected together by things like causation (for Buddhists) or something else (for others). There is no person watching the Dhurandhar movie ; just a series of visual, auditory, and mental experiences. But a series of atomic experiences cannot synthesise to produce a notion of an enduring object. If a person knows that the Hamza of chapter 1 is same as the Hamza of chapter 4, there must be a something about the person (call it the “self”) that lasts through time and enables the identification of Hamza across time. Even within a time instant, to synthesise and infer that the sound that Hamza makes while speaking and fighting is that of the same Hamza that is visually seen, one needs something beyond these senses to make this identification. There must be a self that exists over and across time and senses to identify these objects that endure across time and their qualities that are sensed. There must be lot of different enduring selves to identically identify these enduring objects. In short, there must exist enduring selfs over and above time and sensations to make the identification of objects that exist over and above their qualities and endure across time. This is because : Firstly, the sensual experiences themselves do not speak each other’s language in order to even ask the question, “Have you (the visual sense) grasped the same Hamza as I (the auditory sense) have?”. Secondly, the momentary sensual experiences themselves that last only for instants, cannot connect and infer across time, an object that endures across time. To summarise it shortly, without a self that exists beyond senses and endures across time to synthesise various experiences across time and senses, an object that exists beyond its qualities and endures across time cannot be understood. Momentary fleets cannot comprehend unified and persistent objects. This is the central crux of the Nyāya argument.

If I am only a set of visual and auditory sensations, what would Hamza be for me in the theatre? Given that my self-knowledge is essentially additive, it will be at most a bunch of visual pixels plus sounds or a bunch of visual pixels followed by a bunch of sounds . But distinction O2 tells us Hamza is more than just a sum of his visual and audible qualities. A bundle-self, (that doesn’t persist beyond senses and across time and is just a mere collection of atomic experiences), will be able to infer only similar bundles of auditory and visual properties, and not an enduring object like Hamza beyond those sensed properties. Indeed, it is hardly intelligible how a “set-series” (Sanskrit : *rāśi-dhārā*) sort of mental events without an enduring self can even put together discrete perceptual-data across senses and times as bundles of enduring objects beyond senses and across time.

# **Argument 2 : Subjectivity implies Objectivity**

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Now let us reverse the direction. Suppose we begin by accepting that there is a real, continuous “I” (in the senses of O1-O3), the same person who entered the cinema hall and is still sitting here watching the entire Dhurandhar movie. I remember the early scenes. I compare what I saw with what I am seeing now. I sometimes realise I misunderstood something and correct myself. I am not just a fleeting bundle of sensations; I am a persistent self that lasts through time. For this continuous “I” to actually work, the movie on the screen must be real in the full sense of O1–O4. It can’t be that what exists are just a bunch of visual pixels and sound wavelets that is perceived inside my head. Why not?

Because a continuous self can exist only if there is a stable world for it to continuously relate to. Suppose the movie is not really “out there” at all. Suppose all that truly exists are just isolated flashes of visual sensations and bursts of sound occurring inside consciousness moment by moment. Then what exactly makes my present experience different from my previous experience? At first glance, you may think: “The experiences themselves are different.” But different how? Nyāya philosophers point out something very important here. We normally distinguish experiences by referring to the objects they are about. I say:

“I saw Hamza fighting.”  
“I heard Hamza shouting.”  
“I now see Hamza injured.”

Notice that each mental state is identified through an object that is supposed to persist across those experiences. The experiences are different because they are experiences of the same Hamza in different conditions. But if there are no enduring external objects, then all I really have are disconnected mental flashes:

red-pixels  
loud-sound  
moving-shape  
pain-scene  
silence  
another-shape

The central crux of the argument is that :

That which makes these bunches of sensations belong to one coherent movie rather than random disconnected sensations is also the same thing that allows me to identify myself as the same spectator who owns as he runs across all these changing experiences. Episteomological ownership by a persistent self requires an objective framework within which experiences can be connected, compared, corrected, and organised.

Also, I may initially misunderstand a scene in Dhurandhar and later realise: “*Oh! That person in Chapter 1 was actually Hamza in disguise*.” This correction is possible only because:

1.  there exists a stable object/person (Hamza) who endured across
    scenes,

2.  my present perception can be compared against my past perception,

3.  there exists an objective fact about the movie independent of my
    momentary impressions.

Without enduring objects, there is no distinction between appearance and reality, mistake and correction, memory and imagination, continuity and randomness. The self itself, thus becomes unintelligible. Nyāya philosophers argue that even the idea of a “stream of experiences” secretly presupposes objective reality. A stream needs structure. Structure needs relations. Relations need stable terms that persist long enough to be related. If nothing endured outside experience, then my mental life would collapse into a chaotic spray of disconnected sensations with no principled way to organise them into: “my experiences,” “the same movie,” “the same Hamza,” “past experience” vs “present experience” and this hence destroys the continuity of the stream of perceptions. This continuity in my stream of experiences (structured by enduring external objects), the philosophers argue, is exactly what makes me think of my memories belonging to “the same me.” In other words, realism about the self requires realism about objects. A real enduring self must live in a real enduring world. Otherwise, the self cannot remember coherently, distinguish one thought from another, identify errors, compare experiences, or maintain the identity of its thoughts as “its own” across time.

# **Conclusion**

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

In short, a real, enduring self that remembers, compares, distinguishes its experiences, makes mistakes, and corrects them needs a real, stable external world with enduring objects. Without enduring objects that I can re-encounter across time and senses, my “I” loses its grip on its own identity. It cannot build a coherent timeline, cannot connect its experiences, and cannot even understand itself clearly.

This is the Nyāya insight: the four objective notions (O1–O4) and the subjective notions (S1–S3) are logically interlinked. You cannot consistently affirm one set while denying the other. Realism about objects requires a real self that can track them. Realism about the self requires real objects for that self to track, remember, and be right or wrong about. The Buddhists, in their radical consistency, denied both : no enduring objects and no enduring selves. Nyāya, by contrast, defends the full common-sense package: a real world of enduring objects and real, persistent selves who directly know them. This interdependence is not a philosophical trick. It is what common sense has always quietly assumed when we say both “the world is real” and “I am real.” Old Nyāya wine tastes surprisingly fresh in a new cinematic bottle.

The Nyāya philosophers therefore argue that the self and the world stand or fall together. If there are real objects enduring beyond sensations, then there must also be a real enduring subject who identifies and knows them. If there is a real enduring subject, then there must also be real enduring objects which make possible the organisation and intelligibility of that subject’s experiences. The Buddhists chose radical consistency and denied both enduring objects and enduring selves. Nyāya defended both.

And this is why an innocent discussion about watching Dhurandhar in a cinema hall with a friend of mine unexpectedly opened into one of the deepest debates in Indian philosophy.

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

20
